source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure that VPC Flow Logs is Enabled for Every Subnet in a VPC Network

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure that VPC Flow Logs is Enabled for Every Subnet in a VPC Network
input_row.description: Flow Logs is a feature that enables users to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in the organization's VPC Subnets. Once a flow log is created, the user can view and retrieve its data in Stackdriver Logging. It is recommended that Flow Logs be enabled for every business-critical VPC subnet.
input_row.rationale: VPC networks and subnetworks not reserved for internal HTTP(S) load balancing provide logically isolated and secure network partitions where GCP resources can be launched. When Flow Logs are enabled for a subnet, VMs within that subnet start reporting on all Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) and User Datagram Protocol (UDP) flows. Each VM samples the TCP and UDP flows it sees, inbound and outbound, whether the flow is to or from another VM, a host in the on-premises datacenter, a Google service, or a host on the Internet. If two GCP VMs are communicating, and both are in subnets that have VPC Flow Logs enabled, both VMs report the flows. Flow Logs supports the following use cases: • Network monitoring • Understanding network usage and optimizing network traffic expenses • Network forensics • Real-time security analysis Flow Logs provide visibility into network traffic for each VM inside the subnet and can be used to detect anomalous traffic or provide insight during security workflows. The Flow Logs must be configured such that all network traffic is logged, the interval of logging is granular to provide detailed information on the connections, no logs are filtered, and metadata to facilitate investigations are included. Note : Subnets reserved for use by internal HTTP(S) load balancers do not support VPC flow logs. Impact: Standard pricing for Stackdriver Logging, BigQuery, or Cloud Pub/Sub applies. VPC Flow Logs generation will be charged starting in GA as described in reference: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VPC network GCP Console visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/networking/networks/list 2. From the list of network subnets, make sure for each subnet: • Flow Logs is set to On • Aggregation Interval is set to 5 sec • Include metadata checkbox is checked • Sample rate is set to 100% Note : It is not possible to determine if a Log filter has been defined from the console. From Google Cloud CLI gcloud compute networks subnets list --format json | \ jq -r '(["Subnet","Purpose","Flow_Logs","Aggregation_Interval","Flow_Sampling","Met adata","Logs_Filtered"] | (., map(length*"-"))), (.[] | [ .name, .purpose, (if has("enableFlowLogs") and .enableFlowLogs == true then "Enabled" else "Disabled" end), (if has("logConfig") then .logConfig.aggregationInterval else "N/A" end), (if has("logConfig") then .logConfig.flowSampling else "N/A" end), (if has("logConfig") then .logConfig.metadata else "N/A" end), (if has("logConfig") then (.logConfig | has("filterExpr")) else "N/A" end) ] ) | @tsv' | \ column -t The output of the above command will list: • each subnet • the subnet's purpose • a Enabled or Disabled value if Flow Logs are enabled • the value for Aggregation Interval or N/A if disabled, the value for Flow Sampling or N/A if disabled • the value for Metadata or N/A if disabled • 'true' or 'false' if a Logging Filter is configured or 'N/A' if disabled. If the subnet's purpose is PRIVATE then Flow Logs should be Enabled. If Flow Logs is enabled then: • Aggregation_Interval should be INTERVAL_5_SEC • Flow_Sampling should be 1 • Metadata should be INCLUDE_ALL_METADATA • Logs_Filtered should be false.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VPC network GCP Console visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/networking/networks/list 2. Click the name of a subnet, The Subnet details page displays. 3. Click the EDIT button. 4. Set Flow Logs to On. 5. Expand the Configure Logs section. 6. Set Aggregation Interval to 5 SEC. 7. Check the box beside Include metadata. 8. Set Sample rate to 100. 9. Click Save. Note : It is not possible to configure a Log filter from the console. From Google Cloud CLI To enable VPC Flow Logs for a network subnet, run the following command: gcloud compute networks subnets update [SUBNET_NAME] --region [REGION] -- enable-flow-logs --logging-aggregation-interval=interval-5-sec --logging- flow-sampling=1 --logging-metadata=include-all Default Value: By default, Flow Logs is set to Off when a new VPC network subnet is created. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/using-flow-logs#enabling_vpc_flow_logging 2. https://cloud.google.com/vpc/
input_row.impact: Standard pricing for Stackdriver Logging, BigQuery, or Cloud Pub/Sub applies. VPC Flow Logs generation will be charged starting in GA as described in reference: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

The audit control for ensuring that VPC Flow Logs are enabled for every subnet in a VPC Network is appropriate for an automated approach. This decision is supported by the following:

- The audit instructions provide specific Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands that can be used to check the configuration of VPC Flow Logs across subnets.
- The properties for which settings need to be verified (Flow Logs enabled, Aggregation Interval, Flow Sampling, Metadata) can be directly queried through GCP APIs and validated using the gcloud CLI.
- This control involves checking specific network configuration settings, which is a common operation for automated CSPM tools. 

Based on these criteria, automation is feasible and well-supported by existing GCP capabilities.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_vpc_subnet_flow_logs_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

The automated program will perform the following checks:

- **GCP CLI Command:** Use the command `gcloud compute networks subnets list --format json` to retrieve information about all VPC subnets.
- **Properties to Validate:**
  - Ensure `enableFlowLogs` is set to `true` for each subnet.
  - Verify `logConfig.aggregationInterval` is set to `INTERVAL_5_SEC`.
  - Check `logConfig.flowSampling` is set to `1`.
  - Confirm `logConfig.metadata` is set to `INCLUDE_ALL_METADATA`.
  - Ensure `logConfig` does not contain a log filter expression, and if it does, verify it is `false`.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:** A subnet passes the audit if all expected values are correctly configured as per the criteria above.
- **Scope:** Enumerate all subnets in each VPC network across all projects and regions within the GCP organization.
- **Output:** Provide a detailed report listing compliance status for each subnet, identifying those which pass and those requiring configuration adjustments.

This automated approach allows for consistent and efficient verification of VPC Flow Logs settings across potentially large and diverse cloud environments.
step2_review:
---

### 1. **APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated:** The classification as "Automated" is correct. All required properties (Flow Logs status and configuration) are directly accessible via GCP APIs and the `gcloud` CLI.
- **GCP SDK/CLI Capability:** The `gcloud compute networks subnets list --format=json` command retrieves all subnet details, including `enableFlowLogs` and `logConfig` fields. This is fully automatable.
- **Other Automation Options:** While CloudFormation is AWS-specific, GCP alternatives like Deployment Manager or Config Validator could also be considered, but the CLI/API approach is standard and sufficient here.

---

### 2. **TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8/10

- **Correctness of Automation Details:**  
  - The `gcloud` command is correct and will return the necessary fields.
  - The properties listed (`enableFlowLogs`, `logConfig.aggregationInterval`, etc.) do exist and are retrievable.
  - The pass/fail logic is technically sound.
- **Areas for Improvement:**
  - The CIS control only *requires* Flow Logs to be enabled. The additional checks for aggregation interval, sampling, and metadata are *recommended* settings, not always required for compliance. This should be clarified.
  - The check for "log filter expression" is ambiguous. The field is `logConfig.filterExpr` (if present), but the logic "verify it is false" is unclear. It should check for absence or a specific value.
  - The program name (`gcp_vpc_subnet_flow_logs_enabled`) is descriptive but could be more specific if it enforces *all* logConfig settings, not just enablement.
- **Program Name Convention:**  
  - The name is clear for basic enablement, but if the script enforces *specific* logConfig settings, it should reflect that (see suggestions below).

---

### 3. **IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Clarify CIS Requirement vs. Recommendation:**  
  - The CIS control *requires* Flow Logs to be enabled. The specific logConfig settings (aggregation interval, sampling, metadata) are *recommended* for enhanced logging, but not always required for a pass/fail on the control. Consider splitting the check:  
    - **Required:** `enableFlowLogs == true`
    - **Recommended:** logConfig settings (aggregation, sampling, metadata)
- **logConfig.filterExpr:**  
  - The logic for checking a log filter should be explicit:  
    - If `logConfig.filterExpr` exists, ensure it does not exclude required flows (or flag for review).
    - "Verify it is false" is not accurate; the field is a string or absent.
- **API Alternatives:**  
  - For large orgs, consider using the [Asset Inventory API](https://cloud.google.com/asset-inventory/docs/quickstart) (`gcloud asset search-all-resources`) for organization-wide queries, which can be more efficient than iterating projects/regions.
- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  - If only enablement:  
    - `gcp_vpc_subnet_flow_logs_enabled`
  - If enforcing all logConfig settings:  
    - `gcp_vpc_subnet_flow_logs_strict_config`
    - `gcp_vpc_subnet_flow_logs_recommended_settings`
- **Output:**  
  - Recommend outputting both "required" and "recommended" compliance status for each subnet for clarity.
- **Scope:**  
  - Ensure the script handles all projects/regions (may require org-level credentials or project enumeration logic).

---

### 4. **CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is technically sound and feasible with GCP APIs/CLI.
- Minor clarifications are needed regarding the distinction between required and recommended settings.

---

### 5. **FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** MODIFY

- **Modify** the automation details to:
  - Clearly distinguish between *required* (Flow Logs enabled) and *recommended* (specific logConfig settings) checks, aligning with CIS language.
  - Clarify the logic for `logConfig.filterExpr`.
  - Optionally, update the program name to reflect whether strict logConfig settings are enforced.
  - Consider mentioning Asset Inventory API for org-wide scalability.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section               | Score/Decision | Notes                                                                                 |
|-----------------------|---------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation   | AGREE         | Fully automatable via GCP CLI/API                                                     |
| Technical Accuracy    | 8/10          | Correct, but needs clarity on CIS requirements vs. recommendations and filter logic    |
| Improvements          | -             | Clarify required vs. recommended, filterExpr logic, program name, consider Asset API  |
| Confidence            | HIGH          | Approach and technical details are fundamentally sound                                |
| Final Recommendation  | MODIFY        | Update to clarify compliance logic and naming                                         |

---
